segments.s643-g49: سگ و کبک are fronted nouns — here the words/labels the king adds. بفزود is the 3rd-person singular past of افزودن with the old prefix ب-, meaning “he added.” بر + گفت is a prepositional phrase where بر = “on/upon/to” and گفت is a noun “saying/words” (not a finite verb). شاه is the subject placed after the verb, a common poetic inversion: “the king added (these) to his words.”
segments.s643-g50: شب و روز is a temporal phrase “day and night.” پویان is an active participle/plural meaning “moving/roaming, seeking,” describing an implied group. به دزدی uses به to mark purpose/activity (“for/in theft”), and به راه = “on the road” (به marking motion/location). The segment describes those people roaming the roads day and night to steal.
segments.s643-g33: This clause is basic Persian word order with indirect object/beneficiary first: به هریک ('to each') + یکی گاو ('one cow') + داد ('he gave'). داد is a simple past 'gave'. The coordinating و connects the second item (خری, 'a donkey') as an additional thing given rather than a verb.
segments.s643-g34: ز لوری ('from the Lurs' or 'out of the Lurs') is a poetic prepositional phrase marking source; همی is the classical continuous/emphatic particle (common in Ferdowsi) and precedes the verb ساخت ('made/established'). برزیگری is the verbal/noun object meaning 'cultivation/agriculture' — so the clause says he caused/established farming among (or out of) the Lurs.
segments.s643-g35: همان نیز ('that same also') is an emphatic demonstrative + additive adverb placed before the quantity phrase. خروار گندم هزار is an inverted measure phrase (measure + noun + numeral) meaning 'a thousand khwar of wheat' (classical unit/large quantity).
segments.s643-g36: بدیشان سپرد is a verb phrase: بدیشان ('to them') + سپرد (past, 'entrusted/handed over'). آنک introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'because/that' and بد پایدار is a two-word adjective phrase meaning 'ill-lasting' or 'not durable' (بد here functioning adverbially/adjectivally to negate/qualify پایدار).
segments.s643-g37: بدان تا is a classical connective: بدان ('with that' / 'thereupon') + تا introducing a purpose/result clause ('so that'). بورزد is a 3rd-person verb (classical/polite/habitual form of ورزیدن) meaning 'may practice' / 'cultivate' (here purposeive). به گاو و به خر are instrumental/means phrases ('with an ox and with a donkey').
segments.s643-g38: ز گندم کند is literal 'from the wheat he makes/does' (کند here = 'makes/produces'). تخم و آرد are the two results: 'seed and flour'. به بر is a short locative/benefactive phrase ('for/onto them' or 'for their store/use'); overall the clause says they should make seed and flour out of the wheat.
segments.s643-g39: کند is the (habitual/general) verb 'does/shows'. پیش ('in front of / before / in the presence of') marks the person to whom the action is shown; درویش is the noun 'dervish' and رامشگری is the abstract noun 'gentleness/mercy/soothing action' serving as the object of the verb — 'he shows gentleness before a dervish.'
segments.s643-g40: چو here functions as a conjunction 'as/whereas' (contrasting clause). آزادگان را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker (emphatic). کند is again 'he makes/does', and کهتری is an abstract noun 'inferiority' / 'making lesser' — i.e. 'he causes the free (nobles) to be made lesser' (he humiliates the nobles).
segments.s643-g41: بشد is a narrative/egressive particle meaning “it happened / it came to pass” and introduces the episode. The clause that follows names the actor and the action: لوری (the Lur) + the coordinated objects گاو و گندم and the verb بخورد. بخورد here functions as a 3rd-person singular past/perfective action ‘ate’ in Ferdowsi’s older style.
segments.s643-g42: بیامد is a simple past ‘came’. سر سال is an idiomatic genitival phrase ‘the head/top of the year’ = ‘the turn/beginning of the year’. رخساره زرد is a predicative NP ‘complexion pale’ (زرد here ‘pale/wan’), so the whole line says that at the year's turning his face became pale.
segments.s643-g43: بدو is the prepositional phrase ‘to him’ (marking the indirect object). گفت شاه is the verb+subject order often found in classical Persian (‘said the king’). این نه کار تو بود is a copular clause: این نه (this not) + کار تو (your business) + بود (was), i.e. ‘This was not your job.’
segments.s643-g44: This segment lists the activities the king rejects: پراگندن (to scatter/spread) governs the nouns تخم و کشت (seed and sowing/cultivation). The final درود is another noun coordinated by و. The verbs are nominal/infinitival or verbal nouns; the sequence functions as a list of deeds.
segments.s643-g45: خری ماند is an verbless/nominal clause with خری ‘a donkey’ as subject and ماند ‘remained/was left’ as the verbal predicate. اکنون ‘now’ marks immediacy. بنه is the object ‘the little load/bundle’ and برنهید is an imperative (2nd pl.) ‘place upon’ or ‘put on’ — so: ‘now put the bundle on (it).’
segments.s643-g46: بسازید is an imperative/plural ‘make/compose’. Here it governs رود, which in poetic usage is a stringed instrument (not ‘river’). The coordinating و links a second imperative idea: بریشم دهید — ‘give (the) fine cloth’ (دهید imperative ‘give’). The two imperatives form parallel commands.
segments.s643-g47: کنون ‘now’ sets the time. لوری is the subject. از + پاک گفتار is a prepositional phrase ‘from/on account of his pure speech’; اوی is the older possessive/pronominal form ‘his’ (postposited to گفتار), so the NP reads ‘because of his pure speech.’
segments.s643-g48: همی is the habitual/continuous particle (still/continually). گردد here has the classical meaning ‘goes about / wanders’ (an intransitive motion meaning), and اندر جهان is the locative ‘in the world’. چاره‌جوی is a predicative compound ‘seeker of remedy’ — so the verb+predicate mean ‘he roams the world seeking a cure.’
segments.s643-g1: ازان پس is a fused, somewhat archaic temporal phrase meaning 'after that' (از + آن + پس). به هرسو is a directional prepositional phrase 'to/into every direction'. یکی نامه کرد is a verb phrase with an implied subject ('he') where یکی ('one') quantifies نامه ('a message' or missive) and کرد is the past tense 'did/sent' (here 'sent'). The order is temporal → direction → object + verb, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s643-g2: به جایی که introduces a goal 'to the place that/where…' and که is the relative pronoun 'which/where'. درویش بد جامه کرد is a relative clause with کرد in the simple past 'he put on / made' and جامه as the object 'garment'; بد modifies جامه 'bad/coarse garment' i.e. 'dressed poorly'.
segments.s643-g3: بپرسید is an imperative (2nd-person plural/polite) 'ask!' or a narrative command 'have them ask'; it is followed by a relative/adverbial clause هرجا که … کیست 'wherever there is …'. بی‌رنج is a compound adjective 'without hardship / untroubled' and کیست is the copula 'is' with کی 'who'. The clause asks people to inquire at every place about who is untroubled.
segments.s643-g4: به هرجای is another locative/directional phrase 'at every place'; the predicate follows in typical Persian order: noun phrase درویش و بی‌گنج 'a dervish and lacking treasure' followed by the copula کیست 'who is'. بی‑ is the negative prefix attached to گنج 'treasure' making 'treasureless'.
segments.s643-g5: ز کار جهان is an ablative/partitive phrase 'about/from the affairs of the world' (ز = from/about). یکسر is an adverb 'completely/entirely' modifying آگه کنید. آگه کنید is an imperative plural 'make (someone) aware' or 'inform (me/us)'; the object (me) is implicit or appears in the next segment.
segments.s643-g6: دلم را is the object 'my heart' (دل + possessive suffix -م) with the definite object marker را. سوی روشنی ره کنید is a causative/impositive structure: سوی 'toward', روشنی 'light/clarity', ره 'path/way', and کنید 'make/do' (imperative plural) meaning 'set/turn my heart toward the path of light.' Note: the separate token 'م' is the possessive suffix '-my' (not listed among the provided senses).
segments.s643-g7: بیامدش is a past intransitive verb with the enclitic -ش 'to him' (بیامد + -ش) meaning 'it came to him / he received'. پاسخ is the subject/what came, and ز هر کشوری is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from every country/region' specifying source.
segments.s643-g8: This continues the source list with a parallel coordination: ز هر نامداری و هر مهتری 'from every renowned person and every preeminent/elder/chief'. Each noun is introduced by ز هر and joined by و; the phrase modifies the origin of the پاسخ.
segments.s643-g17: تهی‌دست is a noun phrase 'the empty‑handed / impoverished' serving as the clause subject. بی‌رود is an adjectival/qualifying phrase placed after the subject meaning literally 'without a river' (poetically 'deprived of water/resources'); the coordinating و links the next verb phrase. گل here is used concretely as 'mud/clay' rather than 'flower', and می‌خورد is the imperfective/present form 'he/they eat' (third‑person singular in classical poetic narrative but generalizing to 'the poor eat').
segments.s643-g18: توانگر is the noun 'the wealthy / rich (person)'. همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/truly' placed before the verb for force. ندارد is present third‑person 'has not' (negation of داشتن), and خرد is the direct object 'wisdom' — so the clause says 'the rich truly have no wisdom.'
segments.s643-g19: بخندید is a past (narrative) verb 'laughed' (here with the nuance 'laughed at'). زان is a demonstrative/adverbial 'from/at that' governing نامه 'letter' (direct object). بیدار modifies شاه and follows it in the familiar poetic order to mean 'the king who was awake/alert' (شاه is the clause subject). The word order places the verb before object and subject, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s643-g20: This clause is compact and somewhat elliptical: هیونی is 'a beast/monster' (object), برافگند is the past verb 'threw off / set loose / dispatched'. پویان is a noun meaning 'the moving/active ones' (here 'the riders/men who set out') and به راه is a destination phrase 'on the road / en route'. In this poetic sequence the verb can govern the object placed before it, and the following nominal phrase (پویان به راه) indicates who then went on their way — i.e. the beasts were let loose and the riders set out.
segments.s643-g21: به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/near' (here 'near'), شنگل is the place name, فرستاد is the past transitive verb 'sent', and کس is the indefinite object 'someone' placed after the verb. Word order (prepositional phrase — verb — object) reflects the flexible, often inverted order of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s643-g22: چنین گفت is the stock formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus'), introducing reported speech. کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly; شاه is 'king' and فریادرس is a compound noun 'rescuer / succorer' used here as a form of address ('O king, helper' / 'O king, savior'). The whole segment is the reported utterance: 'Thus he said, "O king, rescuer!"'.
segments.s643-g23: ازان is a prepositional/partitive particle 'from those / of those', لوریان is the plural 'the Lurs' (a people), and برگزین is an imperative 'select / choose' (second person imperative in poetic diction). The line orders the source first ('from those Lurs') and then the command and quantity.
segments.s643-g24: نر و ماده is the coordinated pair 'male and female' (here implying stallions and mares or men and women of fighting capacity). بر is the preposition 'on/upon' or 'with', and زخم بربط is a poetic collocation: زخم here functions figuratively as 'stroke/beat' and بربط is the barbat (a lute) — together 'to the beat/strain of the barbat'. سوار is used adjectivally/participially 'mounted' or as 'riders'. The whole phrase describes the selected ten thousand as male and female, mounted and accompanied by the lute's beat (a poetic way to describe their being marshalled/arrayed).
segments.s643-g9: 'که' introduces the subordinate/relative clause with a purposive/nominal force ("that/so that"); 'آباد' is an adjective meaning "prosperous/inhabited" describing the earth; 'بینیم' is 1st person plural present-subjunctive/optative (می‑ + بین) meaning "may we see"; 'روی زمین' is a frozen phrase "the face/surface of the earth" functioning as the direct object of 'بینیم'.
segments.s643-g10: 'به هرجای' = "to/into every place/wherever" (prepositional phrase giving the domain); 'پیوسته شد' is a passive/resultative-like phrase (پیوسته + شد) meaning "was/has been joined/attached" or "spread/continued" — the auxiliary شد follows the participial/adverbial پیوسته in poetic order; 'آفرین' here is a noun meaning "praise/acclamation," so the clause says "praise was joined/rose in every place."
segments.s643-g11: 'مگر' here marks an exception ("except, save"); 'مرد درویش' is the noun phrase "a poor/dervish man"; 'کز' is the archaic relative "who/that" introducing the relative clause whose verb follows (بنالد); 'شهریار' is the referent connected into that clause (here the addressee/target in the relative clause: he laments in relation to the king).
segments.s643-g12: 'بنالد' (poetic simple present/imperfective) means "he moans/laments"; 'همی' is a habitual/continuous marker ("always/still"); 'از' here introduces cause ("because of"); 'بد روزگار' = "ill/unfavourable fate/times" (بد qualifying روزگار). The whole segment states that this dervish constantly laments because of bad fortune.
segments.s643-g13: 'که' again introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ("for/that"); 'چون' here functions temporally — "when/once"; 'می‌گسارد' (mi‑ + گسارد) is 3rd person singular present imperfective meaning "he scatters/disperses" (here implied: spends or scatters wealth); 'توانگر' is the subject "the wealthy man"; 'همی' marks habitual/action in progress ("always"). Note poetic inversion: verb appears before the subject.
segments.s643-g14:
'به سر' = "on (his) head" (به + سر, location); 'بر' is the preposition "upon/on" reinforcing the locative relation in poetic diction; 'ز' means "of/from" (material or source); 'گل' here means "flower" (so "of flowers"); 'دارد' is the verb "has/wears"; 'افسر' is "helmet/crown/diadem" — together: "he has/wears a helmet (diadem) of flowers upon his head"; 'همی' again gives habitual sense.
Word order is poetic: locative phrase precedes the verb 'دارد'.
segments.s643-g15: 'به آواز' = "by/to the voice/sound of" (prepositional phrase marking the stimulus); 'رامشگران' is the plural agent "peacemakers/placifiers"; 'می‌خورند' is used idiomatically (lit. "they eat") to mean "they are affected by/accept/heed" — here it conveys that people submit to or are swayed by the peacemakers' voice. This is an idiomatic, poetic use of خوردن.
segments.s643-g16: 'چو' = "when/as" introducing the subordinate clause; 'ما مردمان' = "we, the people" (ما = we; مردمان = people); 'را' is the definite/object marker attaching to the object phrase; 'به کس' = "as/to/for anyone" (here meaning "as anyone/of any account"); 'نشمرند' is the present subjunctive/negative 3rd person plural of 'شمار' (شماریدن) meaning "they do not count/consider/reckon". The clause says that in such times they do not count us people as anyone of consequence.
segments.s643-g25: به ایران فرستش = a prepositional phrase + verb with an object suffix. به ایران 'to/into Iran' (destination). فرستش is an older/poetic form meaning 'he sent him' (فرست + the enclitic -ش = 'him/it'). که introduces purpose/result: 'so that'. رامشگری is a verbal noun meaning 'peacemaking/conciliation' and states the intended role.
segments.s643-g26: کند is the verb 'do/make' in the dependent clause introduced by که and should be read as a jussive/subjunctive 'may do' or simple present 'does' here: 'so that he may do / act as...'. پیش is the preposition 'before/in front of / in the presence of'. هر is the universal determiner 'every/each'. کهتری = 'the lesser/ inferior (one)'; بهتر = 'the better/superior (one)'. The final ی is the clitic/copy of the copula (poetic) linking بهتر as 'the better one'. Together: 'to reconcile every inferior with the superior.'
segments.s643-g27: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. برخواند is a past/action verb here meaning 'read aloud' or 'read through'. آن نامه 'that letter' is the object. شنگل is the proper name (the subject) and تمام qualifies the reading as complete: 'when Shangal read that whole letter.' Note the poetic inversion: verb appears before object/subject.
segments.s643-g28: گزین کرد is the two-word past tense 'he chose/selected' (گزین 'choose' + کرد 'did' as past auxiliary). زان = 'from that / from those', marking origin or subset. لوریان = 'the Lurs' (the people selected from). به نام literally 'by name' (i.e. selecting/naming them individually).
segments.s643-g29: به ایران فرستاد = 'he sent (him) to Iran' (فرستاد = past tense 'sent'). نزدیک شاه is an adverbial phrase 'near the king / close to the king' describing where he was placed; poetic order places the destination before the locative qualifier.
segments.s643-g30: چنان کان بود is a descriptive clause 'he was such/of such a sort' (چنان = 'such', کان an archaic particle meaning 'so/as such', بود = 'was'). در خور is a set phrase meaning 'befitting / fitting for' (در + خور), and نیک‌خواه is the noun 'a well-wisher / kind-hearted person'. Together: 'he was of such a nature as to befit a benevolent/well‑wishing man.'
segments.s643-g31: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. لوری 'the Luri (man)' is subject; بیامد is past 'came'. به درگاه شاه is the prepositional place phrase 'to the king's court / into the royal presence' (درگاه here = royal court).
segments.s643-g32: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/said' (polite/formal). تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/result 'that / so that'. برگشادند is the third-person plural past 'they opened' (بر + گشاد + ending -ند), and راه 'the way/road' is the object: 'he ordered that they open the way.'

sentences.s643-s25: The king added “dog and partridge” to his words; day and night they roamed the roads, engaged in theft.
sentences.s643-s17: He gave each one a cow and a donkey, and from the Lurs he set up cultivation.
sentences.s643-s18: He likewise handed over to them that same thousand khwar of wheat, for it was not long-lasting.
sentences.s643-s19: He did this so that they might cultivate with ox and donkey, and from the wheat make seed and flour for their use.
sentences.s643-s20: He showed gentleness before a dervish, while he made the nobles feel inferior.
sentences.s643-s21: It happened that the Lur ate cow and wheat; when the year came round his complexion turned pale.
sentences.s643-s22: The king said to him, “This was not your work — scattering seed, sowing, and greeting.”
sentences.s643-s23: A donkey remained; now put the pack on it. Make (a) rud (stringed instrument) and give (the) fine cloth.
sentences.s643-s24: Now the Lur, because of his pure speech, goes about the world seeking a remedy.
sentences.s643-s1: After that he sent one message in every direction to the place where the dervish had put on coarse garments.
sentences.s643-s2: Ask everywhere who is without hardship; at every place who is a dervish and without treasure.
sentences.s643-s3: Make me fully aware of the affairs of the world; set my heart on the path toward the light.
sentences.s643-s4: An answer came to him from every country, from every notable and every chief.
sentences.s643-s9: The penniless, lacking a stream, eat mud; the rich, truly, lack wisdom.
sentences.s643-s10: The alert king laughed at that letter; he loosed the beast and the riders set out on the road.
sentences.s643-s11: He sent someone up near Shangal; that man said, 'O king, savior!'
sentences.s643-s12: From those Lurs, choose ten thousand — males and females — mounted to the beat of the barbat.
sentences.s643-s5: that we may see the face of the earth prospering; praise was joined to every quarter.
sentences.s643-s6: Except for the poor dervish who continually laments to the sovereign because of ill fortune.
sentences.s643-s7: for when the rich man scatters (his wealth) he always wears a crown of flowers upon his head.
sentences.s643-s8: They are moved by the voice of peacemakers; when they do this they do not reckon us people as anyone.
sentences.s643-s13: He sent him to Iran so that he might act as a peacemaker, reconciling every inferior with the superior.
sentences.s643-s14: When Shangal had read that entire letter, he selected from those Lurs by name.
sentences.s643-s15: He sent him to Iran, close to the king; he was of such a nature as befitted a well-wisher.
sentences.s643-s16: When the Luri came to the king's court, he commanded that the way be opened.
